title: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk at Angkor Wat - Lucas Varro
highlights: Churning of the Ocean of Milk unfolds at Angkor Wat not as illustration, but as cosmology fixed in stone. Carved along the southern wing of the eastern gallery of the third enclosure, extending more than forty-eight metres, the relief presents a universe under strain—its forces bound in tension, its equilibrium threatened, its renewal uncertain. title: Angkor Wat Stories in the Bas-Reliefs: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk and Depictions of Devis - Angkor Grace
highlights: Hidden Stories in the Bas-Reliefs: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk and Depictions of Devis Angkor Wat Stories, One of the most fascinating and symbolic stories depicted in the bas-reliefs of Angkor Wat is the Churning of the Ocean of Milk. This story, drawn from Hindu mythology, is a cosmic tale of the gods (Devas) and demons (Asuras) who, in a fierce battle for immortality, churn the ocean to extract the amrita—the elixir of life. In the Angkor Wat bas-reliefs, this scene comes to life with great detail. The gods and demons are shown pulling a massive serpent, Vasuki, wrapped around Mount Mandara, using it as a rope to churn the ocean. As the churning progresses, it releases not only the precious nectar but also a host of divine treasures, including the goddess  ... Lakshmi, as mentioned, is prominently featured, symbolizing wealth, beauty, and abundance.

title: An Epic In Stone: Angkor Wat's Churning Of The Ocean Of Milk
highlights: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk, a story of gods (the Devas) and demons (the Asuras) working together to recover the lost elixir of immortality. It is this scene that captured the imagination of the Khmer builders of Angkor Wat, who depicted it in an intricate 50 meter stone carving of 88 gods and 92 demons, all working together to attain immortality. 900  ... It is a dynamic work of art. 88 Devas and 92 Asuras, all life-size, struggle to recover the lost elixir of immortality.
